data.title: Ares targets 16%-20% FRE CAGR in 2026 while deploying $158B of available capital
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ares Management Corporation (ARES) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “Our AUM increased 18% year-over-year to $644 billion, and our fee-paying AUM increased 19% to $400 billion.” (Co-Founder, CEO & Director Michael Arougheti)

  • “We are on track for another record year of fundraising as we raised $30 billion of gross capital in Q1, which is our highest ever first quarter and that's up 46% compared to last year's record first quarter.” (CEO Arougheti)

  • “Our available capital continues to expand... and it now stands at over $158 billion.” (CEO Arougheti)

  • “Within U.S. direct lending, equity flows into our non-traded BDC have moderated relative to prior periods, while fund performance and underlying credit fundamentals remain strong.” (CEO Arougheti)

  • “Quarterly management fees exceeded $1 billion for the first time in our firm's history and increased 22% compared to the prior year period.” (Partner & CFO Jarrod Phillips)

  • “Fee-related earnings were $464 million in the quarter, increasing 26% year-over-year.” (CFO Phillips)

  • “We held the final close for ASOF III... raising over $8.3 billion of equity commitments and nearly $10 billion, including related transaction vehicles.” (CEO Arougheti)

  • “We expect to complete the fundraise in the second quarter at its hard cap as the fund is already meaningfully oversubscribed.” (CEO Arougheti)

  • “We've sized the third-party market opportunity at around $900 billion” for data centers. (Partner & Co-President Blair Jacobson)

Outlook

  • “As we look ahead, we remain confident that we're on track to meet our financial objectives for the year.” (CFO Phillips)

  • “In conclusion, for the year 2026, we're on track with our longer-term goals of generating compound annual growth of 16% to 20% in FRE, 20% to 25% in realized income and 20% in dividends.” (CFO Phillips)

  • “We anticipate continued FRE margin expansion, and we expect to be within the upper end of our 0 to 150 basis point annual target this year.” (CFO Phillips)

  • “We also have some exciting structural enhancements to our main fund series... The fourth vintage in the series will be a fully levered fund and we plan to launch a new unlevered evergreen U.S. senior direct lending core product.” (CEO Arougheti)

  • “We anticipate a first close late third quarter or early fourth quarter of this year.” (CEO Arougheti)

  • “Yes, we have not changed our guidance.” (CEO Arougheti)

Financial results

  • “FRE margin expanded 90 basis points year-over-year to 42.4%.” (CFO Phillips)

  • “We generated $75 million in realized net performance income, an 84% increase over the year ago period.” (CFO Phillips)

  • “Realized income for the quarter was $503 million, representing growth of 24% year-over-year.” (CFO Phillips)

  • “After-tax realized income per share was $1.24, up 14% compared to the prior year period.” (CFO Phillips)

  • “Fee-related performance revenues totaled $20 million in the quarter, which were driven by APMF.” (CFO Phillips)

  • “Our tax rate in the quarter totaled 13.5%, just above the midpoint of our 11% to 15% expected range for the year.” (CFO Phillips)

  • “Over the last 12 months, we generated time-weighted returns of approximately 12% to 15% in U.S. direct lending strategies... and over 20% in APAC credit.” (CFO Phillips)

Q&A

  • Craig Siegenthaler, BofA Securities: Asked about “evolving demand dynamics” across institutional, insurance, and retail private credit as retail funds decelerate; Co-Founder, CEO & Director Arougheti: “If we originate a senior secured loan... each of those three pools will get to participate,” and “if you are beginning to see slowing inflows or increased redemptions... those assets will find their ways into other funds and therefore, will not have an impact on our profitability.”

  • Alexander Blostein, Goldman Sachs: Asked where deployment is picking up and whether non-traded BDCs were the “incremental buyer”; CEO Arougheti: “We saw a little bit of a slowdown in the U.S. direct lending part of the business,” but called out strength in “infra and real estate... European direct lending... Secondaries and structured solutions,” adding “the pipeline is reengaged” in U.S. direct lending.

  • Steven Chubak, Wolfe Research: Asked about wealth appetite outside credit and confidence in the “$125 billion” target; CEO Arougheti: “While the U.S. private credit funds are seeing slowing demand, we're seeing increasing demand elsewhere,” and on the target, “Yes, we have not changed our guidance.”

  • Patrick Davitt, Autonomous: Pressed on timing for direct lending “shadow pipeline” to show in “hard numbers”; CEO Arougheti: “The aggregate pipeline across the firm is at a record level and that the direct lending pipeline is increasing in momentum,” citing prior “pause” dynamics and adding “I can't guarantee that that's the case.”

  • William Katz, TD Cowen: Asked about realizations cadence and 2027 margin implications; Partner & CFO Phillips: “The hardest thing for us is to pick the exact quarter,” and on margins, “we expect to still be well within that 0 to 150 guidance.”

  • Bart Dziarski, RBC Capital Markets: Asked about secondaries acceleration; CEO Arougheti: “The GP-led part of the market represents half, if not more, of the current deployment opportunity,” and said the segment looks “least well-capitalized” with “about a one-to-one relationship” between annual deployment and industry dry powder.

  • Kenneth Worthington, JPMorgan: Asked about Europe direct lending deployment; CEO Arougheti: “The deployment there has been quite robust,” adding “pipelines in Europe are as healthy as they are here.”

  • Michael Brown, UBS: Asked about forward-looking software stress testing and positioning; CEO Arougheti: “This is going to play out slowly over time,” and said Ares is “very confident in the quality of the software book,” while also “exit[ing] some names where maybe we have less conviction.”

  • Wilma Burdis, Raymond James: Asked for more detail on data centers and total market size; Partner & Co-President Jacobson: “We've been investing in the digital space broadly for the past 10 or 15 years,” and described a “multitrillion dollar market opportunity,” while CEO Arougheti added it is also about “GPUs, it's power and energy.”

  • Daniel Fannon, Jefferies: Asked if institutional demand differs by geography; CEO Arougheti: “It's pretty broad-based,” while highlighting “a consolidation theme” where large institutions are “doing more with fewer GP partners.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to pressing on retail/wealth flows, software/AI risk, and timing of direct lending reacceleration, including “you can't really see that in the hard numbers” (Patrick Davitt, Autonomous) and requests to “fine tune” guidance (Benjamin Budish, Barclays).

  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and largely confident in Q&A, with repeated emphasis on resilience and diversification, including “we're excited and confident about the opportunities ahead” (Co-Founder, CEO & Director Arougheti) and “we remain confident that we're on track” (Partner & CFO Phillips).

  • Versus the prior quarter, management again leaned into confidence on credit fundamentals and software exposure; in Q4 2025, CEO Arougheti said “we see no change to our earnings growth outlook from AI risks,” while in Q1 2026 he added third-party work concluding “86% of the portfolio with low risk of potential AI disruption.”

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 2026 emphasized fundraising momentum and record first-quarter fundraising (“$30 billion of gross capital in Q1”), while Q4 2025 emphasized full-year records (“record $113 billion in total fundraising for the year”). (Co-Founder, CEO & Director Arougheti)

  • Q1 2026 added more specificity on wealth redemption mechanics and potential firmwide impact, including “it could impact our FPAUM by approximately 1% annually” under a stated stress scenario. (CEO Arougheti)

  • Q4 2025 framed 2026 as “our most significant year yet for the realization of some of our European-style performance fees,” while Q1 2026 commentary shifted to seasonality and cadence, with CFO Phillips emphasizing quarter selection difficulty: “the hardest thing for us is to pick the exact quarter.”

  • Q1 2026 broadened data-center market framing via the GCP/Ada capability and market sizing (“around $900 billion” third-party opportunity), building on Q4 2025’s view that “digital infrastructure [will] be a key contributor to our business in 2026 and beyond.” (Partner & CFO Phillips; Partner & Co-President Jacobson)

Risks and concerns

  • “Heightened geopolitical issues” and “the Iran War” were cited as amplifying a seasonal slowdown and contributing to a slower U.S. direct lending transaction environment. (Co-Founder, CEO & Director Arougheti)

  • Retail vehicle flow volatility remained a focal point, with management stressing structural liquidity design: “these vehicles are specifically designed to align liquidity with the underlying assets.” (CEO Arougheti)

  • AI/software disruption risk was addressed with portfolio segmentation and third-party review, concluding only “1% of the portfolio was categorized as having high risk of AI disruption.” (CEO Arougheti)

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a quarter of record fundraising and scaling profitability, highlighted by $644 billion in AUM, more than $158 billion of available capital, and quarterly management fees exceeding $1 billion for the first time. Executives emphasized institutional fundraising durability, expanding deployment pipelines (especially outside slower U.S. direct lending early in the quarter), and continued confidence in 2026 objectives, while using detailed disclosures to frame retail redemption mechanics and AI/software exposure as manageable within Ares’ diversified platform.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Catalyst Watch: Jobs report, Disney drama, Citi event, and Mickey D's crafted beverage push
data.description:
Welcome to Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch - a breakdown of some of next week's actionable events that stand out. Read more about the events that may impact stock prices next week.

 

Monday - May 4

 

  • Volatility watch - Car rental stocks Avis Budget (CAR) and Hertz Global (HTZ) are set up for another volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include OneConstruction Group (ONEG), Vistance Networks (VISN), and Bandwidth (BAND). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Oio Group (OIO), Option Care Health (OPCH), and Tractor Supply (TSCO). 
  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Palantir Technologies (PLTR), On Semiconductor (ON), Tyson Foods (TSN), and Pinterest (PINS). Options trading implies a double-digit swing for Firefly Aerospace (FLY) after it reports.
  • Dividend watch - Companies that have an ex-dividend date coming during the week include Blackstone (BX), Citigroup (C), Texas Instruments (TXN), and Energy Transfer (ET).
  • IPO watch - Rare Earths Americas (REA) is expected to price its IPOs and begin trading. The IPO lockup periods expire on blocks of shares of Beta Technologies (BETA) and Exzeo Group (XZO).
  • All week - The China Labor Day break is expected to provide a boost to Macau casino stocks such as Wynn Macau (WYNMF) (WYNMY), Sands China (SCHYY) (SCHYF), MGM China (MCHVF) (MCHVY), Galaxy Entertainment (GXYEF), SJM Holdings (SJMHF) (SJMHY), Melco Resorts & Entertainment (MLCO), and Studio City International (MSC).
  • All day - The Federal Reserve's Senior Loan Officer Opinion Survey on Bank Lending Practices will be released. The SLOOS report will provide an update on the conditions, supply, and demand of bank loans extended to customers in the U.S. Tightening standards can signal stress in the banking system or broader economy, impacting financial stocks.
  • All day - IBM's (IBM) four-day IBM Think will begin. The flagship enterprise tech conference will be focused on agentic AI, with sessions and demos focused on how companies can redesign operations with AI at the core to drive ROI, productivity, and growth.
  • All day - The three-day Milken Global Conference will include panels featuring the CEOs from Gap (GAP), Constellation Energy (CEG), and PayPal (PYPL).
  • All day - Shareholders with both Devon Energy (DVN) and Coterra Energy (CTRA) will vote on the pending merger between the companies.

 

Tuesday - May 5

 

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include AMD (AMD), HSBC (HSBC), Arista Networks (ANET), Shopify (SHOP), Pfizer (PFE), Anheuser-Busch InBev (BUD), Energy Transfer (ER), and Duke Energy (DUK). Options trading implies double-digit swings for Lucid Group (LCID) and Joby Aviation (JOBY) after they report.
  • Fedspeak - Federal Reserve Vice Chair for Supervision Michelle Bowman will speak at the Washington Financial Symposium, and Vice Chair for Supervision Michael Barr is scheduled to speak on banking regulation.
  • All day - American Express (AXP) will hold its annual shareholder meeting.
  • All day - The three-day CoinDesk Consensus conference will begin. Notable speakers during the event include Binance founder Changpeng Zhao, Strategy (MSTR) Executive Chairman Michael Saylor, and Cloudflare (NET) Chief Strategy Officer Stephanie Cohen.
  • 10:00 a.m. The New Home Sales report will be released by the Census Bureau.

 

Wednesday - May 6

 

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Arm Holdings (ARM), Disney (DIS), Uber Technologies (UBER), DoorDash (DASH), AppLovin (APP), Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD), and Novo Nordisk (NVO). Options trading implies double-digit swings for Beyond Meat (BYND) and Fastly (FSLY) after their reports are released.
  • Fedspeak - Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago President Austan Goolsbee and St. Louis President Alberto Musalem are scheduled to speak at separate events.
  • Fedspeak - Bloomberg is expected to publish its OPEC production survey for April.
  • All day - Corning (GLW) will hold an event for investors and analysts at the New York Stock Exchange.
  • All day - The window for Boeing (BA) and Airbus (EADSF) to issue their monthly deliveries reports opens.
  • All day - Shareholders with Penumbra (PEN) vote on the $14.5B buyout offer from Boston Scientific (BSX). 
  • All day - McDonald's (MCD) will add six new crafted beverages to its menu, including three specialized refreshers and three crafted sodas. The new crafted beverage lineup from the nation's largest restaurant chain will be closely watched by rivals such as Starbucks (SBUX), Taco Bell (YUM), Dutch Bros. (BROS), and Dunkin' looking to protect their market share.
  • All day - Anthropic (ANTHRO) is holding a Code with Claude event in San Francisco to highlight new Claude capabilities.
  • 8:30 a.m. Disney (DIS) will hold its earnings conference call. Analysts will be focused on the update on the FCC investigation of ABC, streaming momentum, ESPN/NFL, theme park trends, broader content/AI strategy, and capital allocation plans.
  • Postmarket - Costco (COST) will release its monthly sales report.

 

Thursday - May 7

 

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Shell (SHEL), McDonald's (MCD), Gilead Sciences (GILD), Airbnb (ABNB), and Coreweave (CRWV). Options trading implies a double-digit swing for Coinbase Global (COIN), Rocket Lab (RKLB), and Unity Software (U) after they report.
  • All day - Citi (C) will hold its Investor Day. The event is expected to be a strategy-and-guidance reset for the "post-transformation" bank, with management likely laying out a clearer long-term roadmap for returns, capital deployment, and growth priorities.
  • All day - The Carvana (CVNA) 5-for-1 stock split will become effective assuming that shareholders approve it at the May 5 meeting.
  • All day - The Manheim Used Vehicle Value Index will be released.
  • 8:30 a.m. McDonald's (MCD) will hold its earnings conference call. Lamb Weston (LW) is a major supplier to McDonald's that sees its share price correlate tightly with it on the day earnings are released.

 

Friday - May 8

 

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Toyota Motor (TM), Enbridge (ENB), Sony (SONY), and Brookfield Asset Management (BAM).
  • Fedspeak - The Hoover Institution monetary policy panel will feature Fed speakers Michelle Bowman, Mary Daly, Austan Goolsbee, and Christopher Waller.
  • All day - Shareholders with both Sun Country Airlines (SNCY) and Allegiant Travel (ALGT) will vote on the pending merger between the companies.
  • 8:30 a.m. The March jobs report will be released. Economists expect 49K job additions for the month and for the unemployment rate to remain at 4.3%.

Seeking Alpha Editor Josh Fineman contributed to this article.

data.title: Lazard outlines $500M 2027 private capital advisory revenue target as it plans Lazard CL launch
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Lazard (LAZ) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Before turning to our first quarter results and outlook for the year, I want to start with our announcement of the acquisition of Campbell Lutyens, and the future establishment of Lazard CL, a new private capital advisory unit within Lazard that will serve as our third global business" (CEO & Chairman Peter Orszag).

  • "Along with our existing PCA group, the transaction combines 2 highly complementary advisory platforms that will create the leading primary and secondary advisory business globally, with approximately $500 million in anticipated combined 2027 revenue" (CEO & Chairman Orszag).

  • "Firm-wide adjusted net revenue was $673 million, up 5% compared to 1 year ago" (CEO & Chairman Orszag). He said Financial Advisory had "several transactions move to later in the year" and cited "robust growth in restructuring and liability management, and Private Capital Advisory along with solid M&A performance in Europe" as offsets.

  • "In Asset Management, we delivered net inflows of $9 billion this quarter, the highest level of quarterly net flow in almost 20 years" (CEO & Chairman Orszag), and added that "we continue to believe 2026 will be a year in which investors increasingly reallocate towards emerging and international markets."

  • "Financial Advisory adjusted net revenue was $356 million for the first quarter of 2026, 4% lower than the prior year" (Chief Financial Officer Tracy Farr).

  • "Adjusted net revenue was $309 million for the first quarter of 2026, up 17% from the prior year quarter" (CFO Farr), adding "as of March 31, we reported AUM $259 billion" and noting an Edgewater-related "$78 million noncash gain" in GAAP results that was excluded from adjusted results.

Outlook

  • "We anticipate the transaction closing before the end of the calendar year" (CEO & Chairman Orszag) regarding Campbell Lutyens.

  • "The acquisition is expected to be EPS accretive in 2027 with no synergies assumed" (CFO Farr).

  • "I think we would still guide you closer to a comp ratio for the full year similar to what we had last year, around 65.5%" (CFO Farr), while adding that the company expects its 2026 effective tax rate "to be in the high 20s percent range."

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s framing that Lazard would "anticipate hiring within or above our stated range going forward," management said for 2026, "I think that we will be within our range this year in terms of net adds rather than above it" (CEO & Chairman Orszag).

Financial Results

  • "Revenues reflected management fees of $296 million for the first quarter, 25% higher than the first quarter of 2025, and up 3% on a sequential basis" (CFO Farr), while "incentive fees totaled $11 million" and "average AUM for the first quarter was $266 billion."

  • "During the quarter, we had net inflows of $9 billion, market appreciation of $354 million, foreign exchange depreciation of $3 billion and divestitures of $1.5 billion" (CFO Farr).

  • "Our adjusted compensation expense was $471 million for the first quarter of 2026, resulting in a compensation ratio of 69.9%" (CFO Farr), while "our adjusted non-comp expense was $149 million... a non-compensation ratio of 22.1%."

  • "In the first quarter of 2026, we returned $174 million to shareholders" (CFO Farr) and "yesterday, we declared a quarterly dividend of $0.50 per share."

Q&A

  • Devin Ryan, Citizens JMP Securities: Asked about full-year comp ratio and operating leverage; CFO Farr: "we would still guide you closer to a comp ratio for the full year... around 65.5%" and said Lazard has launched "a long-dated program" on support-function efficiencies with "more details... in the second half of the year."

  • Devin Ryan, Citizens JMP Securities: Asked about Campbell Lutyens network effects; CEO & Chairman Orszag: "we believe... we're pro forma the leader" and described "a network effect, a flywheel effect in both directions" plus a combined "data-rich environment" paired with AI.

  • Alexander Bond, KBW: Asked about Campbell Lutyens mix and white space; CEO & Chairman Orszag: said there was "very little overlap" and highlighted complementary secondaries ("GP transactions" vs. "LP transactions"), asset classes, and geography.

  • James Yaro, Goldman Sachs: Asked about sponsor M&A recovery timing; CEO & Chairman Orszag: said private equity deal activity has a "waiting for Godot" element and added that connectivity to private capital extends beyond PE M&A.

  • James Yaro, Goldman Sachs: Asked about Asset Management flows and fee rate; CEO Lazard Asset Management Christopher Hogbin: "I would not straight line the number from Q1 through the year" and flagged possible "moderation" but said Lazard remains confident on "net inflows for the full year" and that the management fee rate "should stay around this level through the remainder of the year."

  • Brennan Hawken, BMO: Asked about deal price and financing; CFO Farr: detailed "$575 million total noncontingent consideration" with "$460 million" upfront and an "$85 million of performance-based earnout," with settlement optionality in "either a cash-like security or stock."

  • Brennan Hawken, BMO: Asked about acquired economics; CFO Farr: said Campbell Lutyens had "healthy operating margins in the mid-20s percent range" and discussed the acquisition multiple relative to Lazard.

  • Ryan Kenny, Morgan Stanley: Asked whether integration rules out more M&A; CEO & Chairman Orszag: said Lazard will remain "quite disciplined," did not expect traditional asset management acquisitions, and said it is "taking a very active look" at wealth management.

  • Ryan Kenny, Morgan Stanley: Asked about proposed European merger rules; CEO & Chairman Orszag: said "it could be" meaningful and linked the change to improving European growth dynamics.

  • Michael Brown, UBS: Asked about pipeline conversion and slipped deals; CEO & Chairman Orszag: "this is not a quarterly business" and declined "false precision" on timing, while cautioning there is "no guarantee" conflict clearances convert to revenue.

  • Michael Brown, UBS: Asked about synergies; CEO & Chairman Orszag: said 2027 accretion assumed no cost synergies but "I have confidence that there will" be synergies, and CFO Farr pointed to potential "shared service" and real estate efficiencies.

  • Daniel Cocchiara, BofA Securities: Asked how Middle East conflict impacted Asset Management; CEO Hogbin: said Lazard "didn't really see any impact in the flows" and noted institutional investors are "longer term."

  • Brendan O'Brien, Wolfe Research: Asked about private credit stress and timing; CEO & Chairman Orszag: said challenges are "concentrated in the software sector" and more severe where retail money is involved; CFO Farr said Capital Solutions can perform better with "bespoke credit" needs.

  • Brendan O'Brien, Wolfe Research: Asked about cross-border M&A and supply chains; CEO & Chairman Orszag: "large multinational firms are definitively rethinking their supply chain footprint" and described a "new environment" of geopolitical choke points.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, with repeated probing on compensation leverage, deal pricing/multiples, sponsor M&A softness, and private credit stress (e.g., questions on "price paid" and whether comp can improve).

  • Management tone in prepared remarks was positive and expansion-oriented around Lazard CL and Asset Management momentum, while Q&A responses were more cautious on timing and variability (e.g., "this is not a quarterly business" (CEO & Chairman Orszag) and "I would not straight line the number from Q1 through the year" (CEO Hogbin)).

  • Versus the prior quarter’s more internally focused transition messaging (CFO handoff) and broad 2026 growth optimism, this call centered on a major acquisition and included more mechanical detail on consideration structure and more explicit caution about quarter-to-quarter advisory lumpiness.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • The current call introduced the Campbell Lutyens acquisition and Lazard CL, including the stated "approximately $500 million" combined 2027 revenue outlook and an expectation the deal will close "before the end of the calendar year" (CEO & Chairman Orszag); the prior call emphasized Lazard 2030 execution, MD productivity targets, and leadership changes in finance and Asset Management.

  • Hiring language shifted from the prior quarter’s expectation to be "within or above" the 10–15 net MD addition range to "within our range" for 2026 (CEO & Chairman Orszag), alongside discussion that about "40%" of MDs remain in a ramping period (CFO Farr).

  • Asset Management narrative moved from Q4’s focus on overcoming a large sub-advised outflow to Q1’s emphasis on $9 billion net inflows and fee rate stability, with management explicitly warning against annualizing Q1 flows.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Geopolitical uncertainty" and "the path forward in the Middle East" were cited as conditions influencing the advisory environment (CEO & Chairman Orszag), with management also highlighting the fragility of "choke points" and supply chain reconfiguration.

  • Advisory execution risk was framed through timing variability: "Financial Advisory activity can admittedly be uneven from quarter-to-quarter" (CEO & Chairman Orszag), and management acknowledged that conflict clearances do not guarantee announced deals or revenue.

  • Private credit stress risk was discussed as more sector-specific: "the private credit challenges are concentrated in the software sector" (CEO & Chairman Orszag), with management pointing to restructuring, liability management, secondaries, and capital solutions as areas of activity if complexities increase.

Final Takeaway

Lazard’s management positioned the Campbell Lutyens acquisition and the planned Lazard CL unit as a step-change in private capital connectivity, highlighted by an anticipated $500 million combined 2027 revenue profile and an expectation of EPS accretion in 2027 without assumed synergies, while reiterating that advisory revenues can be lumpy quarter to quarter. Management paired that strategic expansion with cost-discipline messaging (including a full-year comp ratio guide around 65.5%) and pointed to Asset Management momentum—tempered by a warning not to extrapolate Q1 flows—as it framed Lazard 2030 as increasingly supported by diversified advisory levers and active-management demand for global and emerging market exposure.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Church & Dwight reiterates 2026 outlook for 3%-4% organic sales growth and 5%-8% EPS growth amid $25M-$30M inflation headwind
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Church & Dwight (CHD) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “We had a fantastic quarter,” said President, CEO & Director Richard Dierker, adding that “conditions remain dynamic and the consumer backdrop continues to be mixed,” citing “inflation, borrowing costs and geopolitical uncertainty related to the Middle East... contributing significant inflation in commodities and transportation costs.”

  • “Net sales increased 0.2%... organic sales grew 5%,” and “adjusted EPS was $0.95,” said CEO Dierker, while highlighting brand/category performance including: “ARM & HAMMER cat litter consumption grew a robust 6.8% and share increased 0.4 points to reach 24.6%,” and “THERABREATH achieved another quarter of record share gains, 3.5 points to 24.1%.”

  • “Global e-comm also remained a key contributor with online sales now representing approximately 24% of total consumer sales,” said CEO Dierker, while also stating, “Church & Dwight was #1 across all of CPG on total distribution points gained year-over-year.”

  • “Our situation in the Middle East is fluid,” and “we currently are estimating $25 million to $30 million of incremental inflation pressure,” said CFO & Executive VP Lee McChesney, while emphasizing, “As a result of our mitigating actions, we are reiterating our full year 2026 outlook.”

Outlook

  • “We remain on track to deliver full year organic growth of approximately 3% to 4%,” and “we continue to expect reported sales growth to decline approximately 1.5% to 0.5% as a result of the strategic portfolio actions taken in 2025,” said CFO McChesney.

  • “We continue to expect full year gross margin expansion of approximately 100 basis points versus 2025,” said CFO McChesney, while also noting the company expects marketing “at approximately 11% of net sales.”

  • For Q2, “we expect reported sales to decline approximately 1%, with organic sales growth of approximately 3%,” and “an adjusted EPS of $0.88 per share for the quarter,” said CFO McChesney.

Financial results

  • “First quarter adjusted EPS was $0.95, up 4.4% from the prior year,” said CFO McChesney; “organic sales in 1Q were up 5%... with volume growth of 5.3%, partially offsetting a negative price and mix of 0.3%.”

  • “Our first quarter adjusted margin was 46.4%, a 130 basis point increase from 1 year ago,” said CFO McChesney, attributing the year-over-year change to “150 basis points from productivity programs” and “110 basis points from higher-margin acquisitions,” which “offset 190 basis points of inflation and tariff costs.”

  • “Cash flow from operations was $174.8 million,” and “capital expenditures for the period were $31.9 million,” said CFO McChesney.

Q&A

  • Christopher Carey, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division: asked about the “durability” of volume and distribution gains; President, CEO & Director Dierker said, “we had a tailwind of a couple of points” from prior inventory dynamics, and distribution gains are “really just hitting now,” citing “a 7% TDP lift” on a 13-week average and “closer to 10% or 11%” more recently.

  • Christopher Carey, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division: asked about Touchland runway; President, CEO & Director Dierker said tracked data “shows consumption for the quarter is down 20%,” but “including untracked channels, we were up about 12% or 13%,” adding, “we still are going to have double-digit growth for Touchland for the full year.”

  • Anna Lizzul, BofA Securities, Research Division: asked about portfolio/M&A and Touchland channels; President, CEO & Director Dierker said, “On M&A, I'm not really going to comment,” adding leadership spends “an inordinate amount of time” on deals; on Touchland, “The club class did extremely well. Amazon does well.”

  • Rupesh Parikh, Oppenheimer & Co. Inc., Research Division: asked for organic growth by segment; CFO McChesney said, “U.S. in kind of approximately a 3% zone. International is probably approximately 7%... and then SPD is still sitting at about 5%.”

  • Javier Escalante Manzo, Evercore ISI Institutional Equities, Research Division: asked about commodity impacts and the $25M-$30M figure; President, CEO & Director Dierker said, “It is a full year number,” and the headwind is “primarily... diesel and resin and surfactants,” adding the company typically enters the year “about 60% hedged.”

  • Lauren Lieberman, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: asked about pricing tolerance; President, CEO & Director Dierker said, “we have no plans to try to price through this $25 million, $30 million... We're going to go offset it with productivity.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral-to-slightly skeptical, concentrating on the durability of volume, Touchland deceleration, Middle East-driven cost inflation, and pricing elasticity, including questions that pressed on escalation scenarios (e.g., Deutsche Bank asked what would make the “$25 million to $30 million grow”).

  • Management’s tone was positive in prepared remarks and measured/controlled in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing execution and reiteration of outlook, including “we are reiterating our full year 2026 outlook” (CFO McChesney) and “we have no plans to try to price through this” (President, CEO & Director Dierker).

  • Versus the prior quarter’s Analyst Day/Q4 2024 framing around multi-year strategy and leadership transition planning, the current call’s tone was more operational and macro-aware, with more explicit discussion of geopolitics-driven inflation and mitigation sequencing.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The prior transcript emphasized succession (“Rick will be the fourth” CEO, per then-Chairman, President and Chief Executive Officer Matt Farrell) and capability building (e-commerce, innovation engine, international expansion); the current call reflected that transition, with President, CEO & Director Dierker leading and focusing on execution, distribution wins, and brand-level share/consumption performance.

  • The current call introduced a quantified incremental cost headwind tied to the Middle East (“$25 million to $30 million of incremental inflation pressure,” CFO McChesney) and reiterated the mitigation approach (productivity first), while the prior transcript discussed inflation/tariffs more generally within annual planning.

  • E-commerce mix increased versus the prior transcript’s “over 21%” company level (Analyst Day), with the current call stating online sales are “approximately 24% of total consumer sales” (President, CEO & Director Dierker).

Risks and concerns

  • Management cited macro and geopolitical risk: “geopolitical uncertainty related to the Middle East... contributing significant inflation in commodities and transportation costs,” said President, CEO & Director Dierker; CFO McChesney quantified the related headwind as “$25 million to $30 million.”

  • Management outlined mitigation sequencing if costs rise further: “The first stop... is productivity... The second stop... is RGM on promotions... The third stop... would be pricing,” said President, CEO & Director Dierker, while reiterating, “we don't plan on raising prices at this point.”

  • Brand-specific pressure points included OXICLEAN: “OXICLEAN share declined in the quarter as we continue to be impacted by distribution loss,” said President, CEO & Director Dierker, while also stating “trends on OXICLEAN improved throughout the quarter.”

Final takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a volume-driven start that “exceeded our outlook across key metrics” (President, CEO & Director Dierker) while keeping full-year targets unchanged despite a Middle East-related “$25 million to $30 million” inflation headwind (CFO McChesney). The call emphasized distribution-led momentum across core brands (including THERABREATH share gains and ARM & HAMMER laundry and litter performance), with management repeatedly stating it plans to offset current cost pressure through productivity rather than pricing.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Households turning more hawkish on rates - Conference Board survey
data.description:
U.S. households are increasingly skeptical that interest rates will fall anytime soon, according to new data from Deutsche Bank.

The bank’s analysis of this week’s Conference Board survey found that 62.8% of respondents expect rates to rise over the next year—the highest share since August 2023. Meanwhile, only 13.6% anticipate lower rates, the smallest proportion since October 2023.

The roughly 50-point gap between those expecting higher rates and those expecting lower rates reflects how dramatically the rate-cut narrative has weakened among everyday consumers. Higher energy prices from the Middle East war are significantly weighing on consumers' purchasing power.

data.title: Onemain outlines 2026 targets of 6%-9% managed receivables growth while maintaining 7.4%-7.9% C&I net charge-offs
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: OneMain Holdings (OMF) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "Capital generation was $194 million in the quarter," said Chairman, President & CEO Douglas Shulman, adding, "C&I adjusted earnings were $1.95 per share, up 13% year-over-year" and "Total revenue and receivables each grew 6% year-over-year."
  • "Credit performance was very good and continues to track well against our expectations, both for delinquencies and losses," said Chairman, President & CEO Douglas Shulman. "Our 30 to 89 delinquency declined year-over-year improving on last quarter's slight increase" and "C&I net charge-offs were 8.4%, in line with expectations."
  • On newer businesses, Chairman, President & CEO Douglas Shulman said, "Receivables grew 14% year-over-year to $2.8 billion" in auto finance, and in credit cards, "receivables increasing 45% year-over-year to just under $1 billion, and customer accounts are up 40% year-over-year to nearly 1.2 million."
  • On capital returns, Chairman, President & CEO Douglas Shulman said, "Our regular dividend, which is currently $4.20 per share on an annual basis, represents a 7% yield at today's share price," and "In the first quarter, we repurchased 1.9 million shares for $105 million."
  • "We delivered revenue growth, credit performance and capital generation in the quarter that was right in line with our expectations," said Executive VP & CFO Jenny Osterhout, adding, "Funding was once again a highlight as we further strengthened our balance sheet and access markets favorably even in a challenging environment."

Outlook

  • "We are reiterating our 2026 guidance that we provided last quarter," said Executive VP & CFO Jenny Osterhout. "For full year 2026, we expect to grow managed receivables in the range of 6% to 9%" and "We expect C&I net charge-offs in the range of 7.4% to 7.9%."
  • "We expect our full year operating expense ratio to be approximately 6.6%," said Executive VP & CFO Jenny Osterhout, adding, "All of this supports the strong capital generation of the company for 2026 and beyond."
  • On funding costs, Executive VP & CFO Jenny Osterhout said, "Going forward, we expect our funding costs to remain at approximately this level throughout 2026."

Financial results

  • C&I adjusted EPS of $1.95 vs. $1.86 (analysts’ estimate); total revenue of $1.6 billion vs. $1,265,283,900 (analysts’ estimate).
  • "First quarter GAAP net income per diluted share of $1.93 was up 8% from $1.78 in the first quarter of 2025," said Executive VP & CFO Jenny Osterhout, and "Managed receivables ended the quarter at $26.1 billion."
  • "First quarter originations of $3.1 billion increased 3% compared to the first quarter of last year," said Executive VP & CFO Jenny Osterhout.
  • "First quarter provision expense was $465 million, comprising net charge-offs of $512 million and a $47 million decrease in our reserves," said Executive VP & CFO Jenny Osterhout.
  • "Operating expenses were $437 million, up 9% compared to a year ago," said Executive VP & CFO Jenny Osterhout, adding, "we remain confident in the full year OpEx ratio guide of approximately 6.6%."

Q&A

  • John Hecht, Jefferies: "any update on the bank application"? Chairman, President & CEO Shulman: "No updates this quarter... Timing is uncertain, but we remain optimistic... We're having constructive dialogues with the FDIC and the Utah Department of Financial Institutions."
  • John Hecht, Jefferies: trajectory of branches vs. digital? Chairman, President & CEO Shulman: "we do think our branches are a competitive differentiator" and "our branch footprint... shrank from about 2000 to about 1,400... It's gone down about 100 over the last couple of years," while "try to take... the lower value work into either technology and automated into self-service or into our call centers."
  • Moshe Orenbuch, TD Cowen: what is happening in the back book and confidence in second-half improvement? Executive VP & CFO Osterhout: "that back book represent about 5% of the portfolio, and it contributed 14% to that 30-plus delinquency" and "as those loans get older and we start to see them burn off, we should get closer to our historical range."
  • Moshe Orenbuch, TD Cowen: card profitability and return profile? Executive VP & CFO Osterhout: "We did mention we're now profitable" and "If I look at credit cards, it's probably one of the few businesses... where you would have a similar or slightly higher return profile," adding, "you can able to support over time credit coming in closer to a 15% to 17% range."
  • Arren Cyganovich, Truist: allocation between personal loans vs. auto/card and consumer health (oil prices)? Chairman, President & CEO Shulman: "Each loan we make... needs to meet our 20% ROE threshold" and on the consumer, "we're not seeing it show up in our numbers" while "geopolitical tension and the cost of oil... We haven't seen that creep into our book at this time."
  • Arren Cyganovich, Truist: competitive environment in personal loans? Chairman, President & CEO Shulman: "we have a very conservative credit box" and "There's no change in -- from what we can tell in competitive environment... we really don't chase growth."
  • Mihir Bhatia, BofA: gross charge-offs/recoveries and roll rates? Executive VP & CFO Osterhout: "we did see some normalization in those roll rates, but we're not expecting that to continue" and "we saw very strong recoveries in the quarter... from improvements that we made to our internal capabilities."
  • John Pancari, Evercore ISI: back-book assumptions for charge-offs? Executive VP & CFO Osterhout: "you can assume it's approximately 2x, maybe slightly more than 2x what we would have expected in pre-pandemic" with improvement helped by "the good and young loans that we're starting to put on the book."
  • John Pancari, Evercore ISI: state AG lawsuit exposure? Chairman, President & CEO Shulman: "The bottom line is the claims made by the states are untrue, and they have no merit" and "we do not view this as a material matter or one that's going to have any material impact on our business."
  • Richard Shane, JPMorgan: higher gas prices and potential credit box changes; confidence in second-half credit improvement? Chairman, President & CEO Shulman: "we have a 30% stress overlay in our credit box" and "there's nothing in there that says let's put more overlay on now, but we're also not at the point where we want to take the overlay off," adding on guidance confidence, "the answer is no."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative, pressing on regulatory and macro risks and credit durability, including "any update on the bank application" (Hecht, Jefferies) and "how you think about the credit box now" amid "substantially higher gas prices" (Shane, JPMorgan).
  • Management’s tone was confident in prepared remarks and steady in Q&A, emphasizing stability and reiteration of guidance, including "we remain very confident in the strength and trajectory of our business" (Chairman, President & CEO Douglas Shulman) and "We are reiterating our 2026 guidance" (Executive VP & CFO Jenny Osterhout).
  • Versus last quarter, the Q&A pressure shifted toward legal/regulatory (state AG lawsuit) and near-term macro inputs (energy prices), while management maintained similar confidence language, including "we remain optimistic" on the bank application (Chairman, President & CEO Shulman) compared with Q4’s "we think we have a very strong application" (Chairman, President & CEO Shulman).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance language was unchanged, with Q1 reiterating Q4’s ranges: "managed receivables... 6% to 9%" and "C&I net charge-offs... 7.4% to 7.9%" (Executive VP & CFO Osterhout).
  • Strategic emphasis in Q1 added more operating detail on AI and card scaling, including "began piloting an agentic AI tool" in auto insurance recoveries and "We did mention we're now profitable" in cards (Executive VP & CFO Osterhout), building on Q4’s branch AI knowledge tool launch and credit card efficiency commentary.
  • Capital return pacing increased from Q4 to Q1, with Q4 citing "repurchased 1.2 million shares for $70 million" (Chairman, President & CEO Shulman) and Q1 citing "repurchased 1.9 million shares for $105 million" (Chairman, President & CEO Shulman).

Risks and concerns

  • Macro uncertainty remained a stated risk, as Chairman, President & CEO Douglas Shulman said, "the current economic environment continues to have some uncertainty" and flagged "geopolitical tensions and their impact on energy prices" as "the broader risk," while also stating, "unemployment remains low."
  • Credit headwinds centered on older vintages, with Executive VP & CFO Jenny Osterhout saying the back book is "only 5% of the portfolio" but "still accounts for 14% of 30-plus delinquencies" and is "continuing to go delinquent at about a 2x higher rate than we would have expected."
  • Regulatory and legal items were addressed directly in Q&A: on the bank application, Chairman, President & CEO Shulman said, "Timing is uncertain," and on the state AG lawsuit, he said, "we are happy to go to court" and "we do not view this as a material matter."

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a quarter that stayed on plan, highlighting $194 million of capital generation, C&I adjusted EPS of $1.95, and 6% year-over-year growth in both revenue and receivables while maintaining what it called a conservative underwriting posture. Leadership reiterated full-year 2026 targets of 6%-9% managed receivables growth, 7.4%-7.9% C&I net charge-offs, and an operating expense ratio of approximately 6.6%, while emphasizing continued scaling in auto and credit cards, a higher pace of share repurchases, and ongoing monitoring of consumer risk factors such as energy prices and back-book credit performance.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Ryman signals $350M-$450M 2026 capex while raising guidance midpoints after 27% bookings growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ryman Hospitality Properties (RHP) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered a strong start to the year with results that exceeded our expectations despite the complex geopolitical backdrop," said (Executive Chairman Colin Reed), adding, "In our same-store hospitality business, we grew revenue and market share and expanded margin on slightly fewer room nights."
  • "Gaylord Opryland delivered record first quarter revenue and adjusted EBITDAre Gaylord Rockies delivered record first quarter revenue, and Gaylord Palms delivered record revenue and adjusted EBITDAre of any quarter in its history," said (Executive Chairman Reed).
  • "Same-store ADR increased just over 5% year-over-year, more than offsetting lower group occupancy," said (President, CEO & Director Mark Fioravanti), and added, "Banquet Navy revenue contribution per group room night increased more than 6% year-over-year with gains at nearly every property in the portfolio."
  • "Growth group room nights booked in the first quarter for all periods increased nearly 27% year-over-year representing the strongest first quarter production since 2018," said (CEO Fioravanti), adding, "Reflecting our continued focus on premium corporate groups, corporate bookings comprised approximately 2/3 of production."
  • "We were excited to announce just this week a development partnership with the organization behind the NBA Pacers and the WNBA Fever," said (Executive Chairman Reed) about Ole Red Indianapolis, adding, "This announcement marks our third development update this year."
  • "We ended the first quarter with $424 million of unrestricted cash on hand" and "total available liquidity of approximately $1.35 billion," said (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Jennifer Hutcheson).

Outlook

  • "We are raising the midpoints of our guidance ranges to reflect the first quarter hospitality outperformance," said (CEO Fioravanti), adding, "Our outlook for the rest of the year is essentially unchanged from our prior expectations, reflecting measured confidence in our business."
  • "What gets us to the high end of the range is continued strong near-term group business trends including normalized levels of attrition and cancellations" and "continued momentum in leisure," said (CEO Fioravanti), adding, "The low end of the range assumes some hesitation in near-term meeting plan or decision-making, a potential pullback in 2026 meeting budgets and softer leisure demand, potentially in response to higher gas prices."
  • "At the midpoint for the rest of the year, we continue to assume mid-single-digit growth in group rooms revenue and flattish year-over-year leisure performance," said (CEO Fioravanti).
  • "We continue to expect the third quarter to show the strongest revenue and margin growth for the year" and "We expect same-store RevPAR growth to accelerate as the year progresses, especially as the Gaylord Texan room renovation is completed in August," said (CEO Fioravanti).

Financial Results

  • "At the end of the quarter, our pro forma net leverage ratio... was 4.3x," said (CFO Hutcheson).
  • "In March, we completed an opportunistic refinancing, issuing $700 million of senior unsecured notes due 2034" and "redeeming in full the prior 2027 notes," said (CFO Hutcheson), adding it "extend[ed] our weighted average maturity and eliminat[ed] near-term refinancing risk through the first half of 2028."
  • "We continue to expect total capital spending for the year in the range of $350 million to $450 million," said (CFO Hutcheson), adding, "All major projects remain on time and on budget."

Q&A

  • Charles Scholes, Truist Securities: asked about World Cup-related demand for the Dallas-area property and any FIFA-related cancellations; (Executive VP & COO - Hotels Chaffin) responded, "The World Cup is going to be marginally impactful to our Dallas property" and "it will help us on transient rate," adding, "Dallas is seeing a positive impact, and we should see some on ADR as well."
  • Daniel Politzer, JPMorgan: asked whether guidance embeds conservatism on leisure trends and what is being assumed into 3Q; (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Hutcheson) said, "There's no point of caution that are embedded in our full year guidance" and "our approach to full year guidance at this point is measured confidence," while (Executive Chairman Reed) added, "there are some storm clouds in the horizon that we have to be cognizant of."
  • Smedes Rose, Citigroup: asked whether cancellation/attrition was elevated and if it was storm-driven; (President, CEO & Director Fioravanti) said, "if you look at the first quarter by month and you back out what occurred in January during the Winter Storm Fern, attrition was actually lower" and "cancellations... was essentially flat."
  • Aryeh Klein, BMO: asked about 2027/2028 pace and the impact of inventory-management changes; (CEO Fioravanti) said the company is "making inventory available for premium corporate groups" and added, "For 2027, same-store group rooms revenue on the books is up over 3%... and down 1% for 2028."
  • Richard Hightower, Barclays: asked whether strong corporate booking trends were surprising amid macro headwinds; (Executive Chairman Reed) said, "corporate profits are in really good shape" and "we're betting for the rest of this year and next year, it's going to remain that way," while (COO Chaffin) added, "meeting planners have shown up in a major way and spent very well on property."
  • Cooper Clark, Wells Fargo: asked about OEG’s EBITDA growth outlook and management build-out prerequisites; (Unknown Executive) said, "we have perhaps the most robust pipeline of confirmed growth that we've ever had" and cited "a new COO and a new CMO" additions.
  • David Katz, Jefferies: asked whether slightly higher Desert Ridge guidance changes long-term underwriting and capital needs; (CFO Hutcheson) said, "our expectation for the remainder of the year... is a little unchanged" and "we're right on track" on long-term expectations, while (COO Chaffin) added, "We don't see a massive need for capital."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, with repeated probing on macro sensitivity and durability of group demand (e.g., JPMorgan’s question on "conservatism" in leisure, and Barclays on "macro headwinds").
  • Management: slightly positive with measured caution, emphasizing resilience while acknowledging volatility (e.g., (Executive Chairman Reed) said, "we live in extremely volatile times" and referenced "storm clouds in the horizon," while (CFO Hutcheson) said, "all the leading indicators are continuing to remain very resilient").
  • Compared to last quarter: language shifted from Q4’s emphasis on prudence ("a measured view of demand is prudent," said (President, CEO & Director Fioravanti) in Q4) to Q1’s "measured confidence" alongside explicit macro caveats.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance posture: Q4 framed uncertainty with "a measured view of demand is prudent" (President, CEO & Director Fioravanti, Q4), while Q1 stated, "We are raising the midpoints of our guidance ranges" but kept "our outlook... essentially unchanged" (CEO Fioravanti, Q1).
  • Group demand narrative: Q4 highlighted "record room night revenue and ADR bookings production" in December (President, CEO & Director Fioravanti, Q4), while Q1 emphasized conversion into 2026–2028 pacing, including "production... increased nearly 27%" (CEO Fioravanti, Q1) and on-the-books acceleration "from 6.5%... to 7.6%" (CEO Fioravanti, Q1).
  • Entertainment growth messaging: Q4 cited new amphitheater and Category 10 pipeline (Executive Chairman Colin Reed, Q4), while Q1 stressed Ole Red development momentum including Indianapolis with the Pacers/Fever organization (Executive Chairman Reed, Q1).

Risks and Concerns

  • "Extremely volatile times" and potential "rate hikes" tied to oil-price shocks were flagged as external risks, said (Executive Chairman Reed), while management also cited potential "pullback in 2026 meeting budgets" and "softer leisure demand" as downside scenarios, said (CEO Fioravanti).
  • Weather disruption remained a named operational factor: (CEO Fioravanti) cited January impacts from "Winter Storm Fern" and positioned February–March improvement as normalization.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as hospitality-led outperformance driven by pricing discipline and higher-value group mix, alongside a raised midpoint outlook that kept the rest-of-year view intact. Leadership pointed to forward demand indicators—27% production growth, normalized attrition/cancellations excluding January weather impacts, and continued corporate mix emphasis—while pairing that confidence with explicit macro caution. Capital allocation remained centered on a $350 million to $450 million 2026 spend plan and refinancing actions intended to remove near-term maturities through early 2028.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: TPG expects 2026 capital raising to exceed $50B as it targets a 47% FRE margin
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TPG Inc. (TPG) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "TPG entered 2026 with strong momentum following a record year of capital formation and deployment," said CEO Jon Winkelried, adding that "our fee-related earnings grew 36% year-over-year and exceeded $1 billion on an LTM basis for the first time in TPG's history" and "total AUM grew 22% to $306 billion."

  • Winkelried framed the macro backdrop as "the convergence of AI disruption, private credit stress and geopolitical conflict" and said, "We view the current environment as an opportunity, and we have never felt more confident in the positioning of our franchise."

  • On AI and software, Winkelried said, "We've evaluated each of our software companies through a framework based on offensive opportunity and defensive risk" and highlighted that "aggregate bookings in our TPG Capital and TPG Growth software portfolio growing more than 20% year-over-year."

  • On private credit positioning, Winkelried said, "our credit portfolios are healthy" and pointed to TCAP flows: "gross inflows of $193 million and redemption requests of $31 million" and said TCAP "ended the quarter with $4.7 billion of AUM, up 33% year-over-year."

  • CFO Jack Weingart said, "We reported fee-related revenue of $557 million in the first quarter" and "Fee-related earnings for the quarter were $247 million" while "Our FRE margin was 44.3% in the quarter."

Outlook

  • Weingart reaffirmed profitability goals, saying, "We remain confident in our ability to achieve a full year 2026 FRE margin of 47%."

  • Management reiterated the annual fundraising target, with Winkelried stating, "Overall, we remain on track to raise more than $50 billion this year," and Weingart adding, "We continue to expect capital raising to exceed $50 billion this year" while noting it is expected to be "weighted toward the back half of the year."

  • Product roadmap emphasis in wealth and credit continued, with Weingart saying, "we're actively working on launching new products, including a nontraded REIT as well as a multi-strategy credit interval fund," and Winkelried adding that clients "have expressed interest in a TPG multi-strategy credit interval fund, which we plan to launch next year."

Financial Results

  • In prepared remarks, Head of IR Gary Stein reported, "a GAAP net loss attributable to TPG Inc. of $123 million" and "after-tax distributable earnings of $282 million or $0.70 per share" and added, "We declared a dividend of $0.59 per share ... which will be paid on May 26."

  • Weingart said AUM growth was supported by capital raising and value creation: "We ended the quarter with $306 billion of total assets under management" and "Our fee-earning AUM grew 23% to $175 billion" while "AUM subject to fee earning growth totaled $45 billion ... including $33 billion of AUM not yet earning fees" and "we have visibility into approximately $140 million of annual revenue opportunity as this capital is put to work."

  • Weingart highlighted balance sheet actions tied to the Jackson partnership: "We used our revolver to fund $500 million investment in Jackson common stock" and later "issued $500 million of senior notes"; he said, "our interest expense increased to $26 million" and "we had $2.3 billion of net debt and $1.7 billion of available liquidity."

  • On realizations and marks, Weingart said, "We generated $68 million in realized performance allocations" and added, "the value of our PE portfolio declined 1%" while "Our credit platform appreciated 2% in the first quarter" and "Our real estate platform appreciated approximately 2% in the first quarter."

Q&A

  • Glenn Schorr, Evercore ISI: "help us think through the marks in PE" and whether they were "a few specific names" and "how much software related"; Chief Financial Officer Weingart: "we did take multiples down broadly, and it was offset by very strong earnings growth" and quantified, "The impact of multiple reductions was negative $2.4 billion" in TPG Capital and "negative $1.1 billion" in growth; President Todd Sisitsky: "we had two strategic exits ... at premiums to our marks."

  • Alexander Blostein, Goldman Sachs: asked which credit verticals should be "most active" and any "implications on the fee rates"; CEO Winkelried: "the pipeline of opportunities there has never been stronger" for Credit Solutions and highlighted asset-based finance/structured credit as "a very substantial growth area."

  • Craig Siegenthaler, BofA Securities: asked if AI enablement meant cross-fund follow-ons and appetite for software opportunities; Sisitsky: "we do not start to cross and come in from new funds" and described recent carve-outs including "Velotic" and "Optum U.K."; Executive Chairman James Coulter said, "By the end of this year, I think we'll probably be talking about offense" on AI.

  • Kenneth Worthington, JPMorgan: asked how large deals translate into capital markets fees and then, "nothing to call out for 2Q?"; Weingart: "Predicting in one quarter is hard" and responded to the follow-up with "No."

  • Steven Chubak, Wolfe Research: asked about AI-risk review beyond software and whether marks reflected weaker growth; Sisitsky: "it was distinctively just the public marks coming down" and said remaining TPG VIII value was $13.7 billion with "7%" in a "mitigate" category.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly skeptical in spots, led by valuation-mark questions (e.g., Schorr: "I'm going to pick at one issue") and fee/margin probing (Giblat: "I'm just trying to reconcile the potential upside I see here").

  • Management tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks, with Winkelried saying, "we have never felt more confident" and Weingart emphasizing "continued growth and margin expansion" while giving specific guardrails on margin and fundraising.

  • Versus the prior quarter, the current call carried more emphasis on mark-to-market and AI-risk framing in Q&A, while the prior quarter featured more explicit pushback on market skepticism (Q4 Schorr: "your stocks fall like rocks") that was not repeated in Q1.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Profitability mix shifted from Q4 profitability to Q1 loss on a GAAP basis, as Stein reported Q4 "GAAP net income ... of $77 million" versus Q1 "GAAP net loss ... of $123 million," while after-tax distributable earnings were $304 million in Q4 versus $282 million in Q1.

  • FRE margin stepped down from a Q4 peak to a more typical level, with Weingart previously reporting "fourth quarter FRE margin reached a record 52%" and now reporting Q1 "FRE margin was 44.3%" while keeping the same full-year target of "approximately 47%"/"47%."

  • Dividend declaration moved lower sequentially, from Q4 "$0.61 per share" to Q1 "$0.59 per share," as disclosed by Stein on each call.

  • Strategic narrative evolved from Q4 announcing the Jackson partnership structure and minimums to Q1 discussing execution, with Winkelried saying the Jackson partnership was "tracking ahead of our plan" and noting "$2 billion of initial commitments" received into asset-based finance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged uncertainty drivers, with Winkelried citing "AI disruption, private credit stress and geopolitical conflict" and Weingart warning that "current market volatility may impact the timing of realizations across the industry."

  • On valuations, Weingart described the mitigation approach as aligning to markets: "we did take multiples down broadly" and said improved markets would be reflected later: "if market conditions continue to improve, we'll reflect those increasing valuation multiples."

  • On retail-oriented credit vehicles, Winkelried acknowledged, "some retail-oriented credit vehicles are experiencing elevated redemptions," while pointing to TCAP’s reported redemption requests as a counterpoint.

Final Takeaway

TPG management described Q1 2026 as a quarter of accelerating platform activity alongside a tougher mark-to-market backdrop: Winkelried emphasized AI-related portfolio positioning, credit resilience, and a continued fundraising target of more than $50 billion for 2026, while Weingart reiterated a 47% full-year FRE margin goal and detailed balance sheet actions tied to the Jackson partnership, alongside a quarter that included a GAAP loss, lower private equity valuations driven by multiple resets, and ongoing credit and real estate appreciation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Alliant outlines 7%+ earnings CAGR for 2027-2029 as contracted data center demand reaches 3.4 GW
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Alliant Energy (LNT) Q1 2026

Management view

  • CEO Lisa Barton said “2026 is off to an excellent start,” adding that “First quarter ongoing earnings delivered approximately 25% of the midpoint of our full year guidance despite very mild temperatures across our service territory,” and that the company remains “firmly on track to achieve our 2026 earnings targets while executing on our strategic priorities.”

  • CEO Barton highlighted a new large-load contract: “In April, we executed a new 370-megawatt electric service agreement with a hyperscale customer in Iowa with a full load ramp expected by the end of 2030,” and said Alliant “entered into an agreement with a high-quality counterparty to construct a simple cycle natural gas facility” to support that growth.

  • CEO Barton said the company now has “5 fully executed data center agreements representing approximately 3.4 gigawatts of contracted demand with 3 of these projects under active construction,” and framed the scale as “more than a 60% increase in our current peak demand.”

  • CEO Barton tied the data center contracting approach to capital needs and flexibility: “because our more recent electric service agreements are capacity only, the investments required to serve this load are primarily energy storage and natural gas combustion turbines.”

  • CFO Robert Durian reported Q1 profitability: “Yesterday, we announced solid first quarter 2026 GAAP and ongoing earnings of $0.87 and $0.82, respectively,” and said the year-over-year change in ongoing earnings was “primarily due to higher revenue requirements and AFUDC from capital investments at our Iowa and Wisconsin utilities,” offset by higher O&M, depreciation, and financing costs.

Outlook

  • CFO Durian said, “We are reaffirming our 2026 earnings guidance,” while adding, “Our longer-term earnings outlook remains intact,” and “based on our current plan, we expect our compound annual earnings growth rate across 2027 through 2029 to be 7% plus.”

  • CEO Barton guided to more disclosure later in the year on the incremental data center pipeline: “our third quarter earnings call and EEI, we will be providing that full update of our resource plan, which would include providing the generation necessary to support the 370 megawatts, an update on our EPS and growth trajectory.”

  • Compared with the prior quarter, management again used the same long-term framing around growth, with CFO Durian reiterating “7% plus” for 2027-2029, while the current quarter added a specific near-term catalyst in a newly executed “370-megawatt electric service agreement.”

Financial results

  • CFO Durian stated temperature impacts: “Temperatures in the first quarter of 2026 reduced electric and gas margins by approximately $0.04 per share compared to a reduction of $0.03 in the prior year,” and added, “Excluding the impacts of temperatures, electric sales in the first quarter were essentially even year-over-year.”

  • CFO Durian described an item excluded from ongoing earnings: “First quarter ongoing earnings exclude a $0.05 benefit from the remeasurement of deferred tax assets, reflecting updated state income tax apportionment assumptions driven by higher projected electric utility revenues from commercial and industrial customers, including data centers.”

  • CFO Durian detailed Q1 financing execution and remaining 2026 plans: “during the first quarter of 2026, we had parent level and Alliant Energy Finance maturities of $1.1 billion,” which were retired “including a $400 million term loan,” and he said remaining 2026 debt plans include “up to $800 million of long-term issuances, consisting of up to $300 million at WPL and up to $500 million at IPL.”

  • CFO Durian updated equity funding actions: “of the approximately $2.4 billion of expected common equity needs over the next 4 years, we have already raised approximately $1.3 billion through forward equity agreements,” leaving “approximately $1 billion of remaining equity to be raised through 2029,” and added, “A new $1 billion at-the-market program was filed during the first quarter.”

Q&A

  • Shahriar Pourreza, Wells Fargo: asked whether Alliant could provide “EPS disclosures” and a “more definable EPS guidance range” as data center opportunities “start to accrete”; President, CEO & Director Barton responded, “our third quarter earnings call and EEI, we will be providing… an update on our EPS and growth trajectory.”

  • Pourreza, Wells Fargo: asked about “noise in Wisconsin” and whether hyperscalers are shifting to Iowa; President, CEO & Director Barton said, “conversations do continue in Wisconsin,” while noting, “there’s rhetoric that’s out there… spillover… from PJM,” and “we have our customer pledge, making sure that everybody knows that they are not paying for data centers.”

  • Nicholas Campanella, Barclays: asked for simple-cycle cost per kW; President, CEO & Director Barton said, “We cannot disclose the cost due to confidentiality agreements,” and added, “you can expect those to be in line with what you’re seeing in the marketplace today.”

  • William Appicelli, UBS: asked about resource mix and MISO accreditation; President, CEO & Director Barton said the mix is “primarily batteries and peakers,” and on timing added, “we’ll have a cleaner line of sight as we get closer to Q3.”

  • Appicelli, UBS: asked CT sizing; President, CEO & Director Barton said, “it basically 1.1 gigawatts,” then clarified “Up to.”

  • Andrew Weisel, Scotiabank: asked in-service date for the new CT; President, CEO & Director Barton answered, “’31.”

  • Stephen D’Ambrisi, RBC: asked whether incremental load extends Iowa’s ability to “stay out” of base rate cases longer; Executive VP & CFO Durian said, “think of it as incrementally better,” but added, “we’re not in a position right now to give you any kind of definitive time frame.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly positive but pressing on quantification and execution risk, repeatedly asking for incremental economics and disclosures (e.g., Pourreza, Wells Fargo: “do we ever get to a point where we could see a more definable EPS guidance range”).

  • Management tone in prepared remarks was positive and confident on execution cadence and strategy (President, CEO & Director Barton: “2026 is off to an excellent start”), while responses in Q&A were more guarded on specifics, frequently citing confidentiality (President, CEO & Director Barton: “We cannot disclose the cost due to confidentiality agreements”).

  • Versus last quarter, analyst questioning remained centered on data center timing and Wisconsin siting dynamics; this quarter added more detailed probing on generation sizing, MISO accreditation impacts, and the timeline for combustion turbines.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The current quarter expanded contracted load disclosure from “4 executed ESAs totaling 3 gigawatts” (prior quarter) to “5 fully executed data center agreements… approximately 3.4 gigawatts,” and added a new “370-megawatt electric service agreement” with a ramp “by the end of 2030.”

  • Financing commentary shifted from planned issuances to actions taken: in Q4 the company outlined “up to $1.2 billion of long-term issuances” for 2026, while in Q1 management reported $1.1B of maturities addressed and said remaining 2026 long-term issuances are “up to $800 million.”

  • Regulatory posture remained consistent on “no active rate reviews planned in 2026,” while the current quarter added new docket activity including “1 active Iowa docket for a 720-megawatt natural gas combustion turbine project” and “5 active Wisconsin dockets.”

Risks and concerns

  • Management flagged external process uncertainty around reliability accreditation and interconnection policy, with President, CEO & Director Barton noting MISO is “still working on some of that,” and on FERC policy saying, “We are anxiously waiting… but no line of sight on that.”

  • Wisconsin siting and public pushback remained a topic, with President, CEO & Director Barton referencing “local pushback and moratoriums on new data center developments” and saying the company is “actively addressing countering that” while emphasizing customer cost protections.

Final takeaway

Alliant Energy management positioned Q1 as on-track against reaffirmed 2026 guidance, while emphasizing accelerating large-load contracting, including a new 370 MW Iowa agreement and a total of about 3.4 GW across five executed data center ESAs. Management linked the incremental load to a resource strategy centered on batteries and simple-cycle combustion turbines, with an update planned for Q3 alongside a refreshed Iowa resource plan and an “update on our EPS and growth trajectory,” while maintaining the longer-term “7% plus” earnings growth outlook for 2027-2029.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Barclays turns neutral on risk assets as Hormuz closure persists
data.description:
Barclays Global Chairman of Research Ajay Rajadhyaksha said Friday he has downgraded his outlook on risk assets to neutral, citing accelerating declines in global oil (USO) (BNO) inventories as the cushion that supported his earlier bullish stance erodes.

In a Bloomberg interview, Rajadhyaksha explained that the initial three-week buffer following the Strait of Hormuz disruption has been exhausted. Global inventories are now drawing down at 80M to 90M barrels per week, with outages running at 10M to 12M barrels daily.

“The first three weeks, you still had tankers that had left Hormuz that were landing on shores,” Rajadhyaksha said. “That cushion is gone.”

The strategist had previously maintained a bullish stance, arguing that markets underestimated the buffers held by major economies. He pointed to China’s (MCHI) 1.2B barrels in strategic reserves and the U.S.'s (SPY) status as an energy superpower, along with roughly 700M barrels of inventory built up last year.

Despite his more cautious view, Rajadhyaksha stopped short of recommending outright shorts on risk assets, noting that strong corporate earnings continue to provide support.

He favors the “Mag Six”—the major technology companies excluding Tesla (TSLA)—and two-year Treasuries (SHY) (US2Y), while warning investors away from long-duration bonds (ZROZ) (US30Y) globally.
